title: Deal or no deal, conflict tremors are here to stay
gist: Even as the Iran/Middle East conflict winds down and global oil prices fall from late-May highs, Oxford Economics expects Africa's 2026 full-year inflation to run higher and growth lower than its February baseline, forcing tighter monetary policy and harder fiscal consolidation across the continent.

themes.theme: Africa growth/inflation downgrade despite oil relief

insights.claim: Oxford Economics expects 2026 full-year inflation across much of Africa to come in higher than its February forecast and economic growth lower, despite global oil prices falling since late May.
insights.speaker: Sanele Mjikane
insights.so_what: Fade the 'oil relief = African recovery' narrative; African rates and FX should price persistent inflation, not a clean easing cycle.
insights.quote: full-year inflation will be higher than expected in February, while economic growth across much of the continent will be lower
insights.locator: Whatever happens after negotiations between the US and Iran

insights.claim: A Q2 2026 growth hit followed by a sluggish H2 upturn will keep African economic growth subdued even though lower H2 oil prices ease pressure on household real incomes and fiscal positions.
insights.speaker: Sanele Mjikane
insights.so_what: The H2 oil tailwind is real but insufficient; expect weak growth prints, limiting the upside for African risk assets near-term.
insights.quote: the Q2 hit and a sluggish upturn in H2 will likely keep economic growth subdued
insights.locator: Lower oil prices in the second half of the year

themes.theme: Tighter monetary policy and debt-servicing squeeze

insights.claim: Inflationary spillovers from the Middle East conflict will warrant tighter African monetary policy than anticipated at the start of 2026, feeding higher domestic borrowing costs.
insights.speaker: Sanele Mjikane
insights.so_what: Position for higher-for-longer African policy rates; local-currency bond yields biased up versus February expectations.
insights.quote: Expectations of inflationary pressure will warrant tighter monetary policy than anticipated at the start of the year.
insights.locator: Policymakers will have to contend with the adverse economic spillovers

insights.claim: Higher domestic borrowing costs will stymie African governments' attempts to arrest the rise in debt-servicing outlays.
insights.speaker: Sanele Mjikane
insights.so_what: Watch for widening sovereign debt-service ratios; credit-negative for already-stressed African sovereign issuers.
insights.quote: This will feed into higher domestic borrowing costs, stymying attempts to arrest the rise in debt servicing outlays.
insights.locator: Policymakers will have to contend with the adverse economic spillovers

themes.theme: Fiscal consolidation derailed by fuel subsidies

insights.claim: The cost of fuel subsidies has forced several African fiscal authorities to revisit their current fiscal-year budgets.
insights.speaker: Sanele Mjikane
insights.so_what: Mid-year budget revisions signal slippage; monitor named oil-importing sovereigns for revised deficit targets.
insights.quote: the cost of fuel subsidies has forced several fiscal authorities to revisit their budgets for the current fiscal year
insights.locator: At the same time, the cost of fuel subsidies

insights.claim: Efforts to shield African households from financial strain will reduce consumption-tax revenues, making fiscal consolidation more difficult.
insights.speaker: Sanele Mjikane
insights.so_what: Revenue side of consolidation deteriorates even as spending pressure rises; a double squeeze on African fiscal credibility.
insights.quote: Efforts to shield households from financial strain will also reduce consumption tax revenues, making fiscal consolidation more difficult.
insights.locator: At the same time, the cost of fuel subsidies
